mnemonics.title: A* STAR Power
mnemonics.content: A* = f(n) = g(n) + h(n) — 'Always Start Toward Amazing Result' — g(n) is the cost from start to node, h(n) is estimated cost from node to goal, f(n) is the total estimated cost

mnemonics.title: Best First Bridge
mnemonics.content: Best-First Search uses a priority queue — think 'Best Branches First Build Bright Bridges' — always expand the node with the highest priority (lowest heuristic value)

mnemonics.title: Heuristic Helper
mnemonics.content: Heuristic (h) — 'Helpful Estimate Reveals Intelligent Solutions' — a rule of thumb that estimates the cost to reach the goal

mnemonics.title: Greedy vs A*
mnemonics.content: Greedy Best-First = h(n) only (like a greedy person), A* = g(n) + h(n) (considers path so far + heuristic) — 'Greedy Goes Home, A* Adds Ground Cost'

acronyms.usage: When you need an optimal and complete search algorithm that guarantees the shortest path

acronyms.expansion: Cost from start node to current node n
acronyms.usage: When calculating the actual path cost traveled so far in A*

acronyms.expansion: Heuristic estimated cost from node n to goal
acronyms.usage: When you need to estimate the remaining cost to reach the target
When determining which node to expand next in A* search
When ensuring A* finds the optimal solution

acronyms.expansion: Priority queue of nodes to be evaluated
acronyms.usage: When tracking nodes waiting to be expanded in search algorithms
When tracking nodes that have been fully explored

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a GPS navigation system — g(n) is the distance traveled so far, h(n) is the straight-line distance to destination, f(n) is the total estimated journey time. The algorithm always picks the route with the lowest total estimated time.

Think of a tree where each node has a label showing its heuristic value. You're always plucking the 'lowest hanging fruit' (lowest heuristic) first — like selecting the most promising path immediately.
Picture a lighthouse beam from the goal node — the heuristic illuminates how close you are to the destination. Stronger beam = closer = lower h(n) value = higher priority to explore.

Imagine a rubber band stretched from current node to goal — the heuristic must never stretch further than the shortest possible path. It can be shorter (underestimate) but never longer (overestimate).

analogies.analogy: Like planning a road trip using both distance already driven (g) and estimated remaining distance (h) — you choose routes that minimize total expected travel time, not just the remaining distance (like GPS with traffic + straight-line distance).
Like a tourist who only looks at the map and picks the direction that seems closest to the landmark, without considering how far they've already traveled — may take a longer route.
Like using a treasure map with 'warm' and 'cold' hints — the heuristic tells you which direction is 'warmer' (closer to the goal) so you can make informed guesses instead of random searching.
Think of a hospital emergency room — patients (nodes) are prioritized by severity (heuristic value). The most critical (lowest h-value) gets treated first, even if they arrived later.

Like having a perfect weather forecast for your journey — if your heuristic (weather prediction) never overestimates delays, you'll always find the fastest route. If it overestimates, you might miss a faster path.
